era_context: 1192 CE is in the late 12th century, a period of significant political and military upheaval across Eurasia. Saladin was still alive but in declining health (he would die in 1193). The Angevin Empire under Richard I was under pressure from Philip II Augustus of France. The Khmer Empire under Jayavarman VII was consolidating power. Japan's Kamakura shogunate had just been established (1185) and was consolidating power. The Byzantine Empire was experiencing internal instability. This was a period of major transitions in political power across multiple regions.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Central Asia and the Steppe (limited contemporary records)
Mesoamerica and South America (no written records in European tradition)

events.title: Richard I of England captured while returning from Crusade
events.description: King Richard I of England (Richard the Lionheart) was captured in December 1192 while returning from the Third Crusade. He was captured by Duke Leopold V of Austria near Vienna, allegedly because of a dispute over the division of spoils from the Crusade and Richard's role in the death of Leopold's cousin. Richard's capture was a major political event that had far-reaching consequences for the Angevin Empire. His absence allowed Philip II Augustus of France to make significant territorial gains in Normandy and Anjou. Richard would remain imprisoned until February 1194, when a ransom of 150,000 marks was paid. His capture is attested in contemporary English and Norman chronicles.
key_figures: Richard I of England (King, Duke of Normandy and Aquitaine)
Philip II Augustus (King of France, rival)

sources.name: Itinerarium Peregrinorum et Gesta Regis Ricardi (Itinerary of the Crusaders and Deeds of King Richard)

events.certainty_note: Richard's capture in December 1192 is well-documented in contemporary English and Norman chronicles. The date and basic facts are confirmed by multiple sources.

events.title: Second Battle of Tarain; Muhammad of Ghor defeats Rajput confederation
events.description: The Second Battle of Tarain (also called the Battle of Tarain II) was fought in 1192 between Muhammad of Ghor (Muhammad Ghori) and the Rajput confederation led by Prithviraj Chauhan. Muhammad of Ghor's forces defeated the Rajput confederation, marking a turning point in Indian history. The victory opened the way for Islamic conquest of northern India and the establishment of the Delhi Sultanate. Prithviraj Chauhan was captured and executed (according to some sources). The battle is attested in Islamic chronicles and later Indian historical sources, though the exact date and details are debated. This battle is considered one of the most significant military engagements in Indian history.
key_figures: Muhammad of Ghor (Ghurid ruler, military conqueror)
Qutb ud-Din Aibak (Muhammad's general, founder of Delhi Sultanate)

sources.name: Taj-ul-Maasir by Hasan Nizami, compiled early 13th century
Firishta, History of the Muhammadan Empire in India, compiled 16th century
Prithviraj Raso (Prithviraj's Praise), oral tradition compiled later

events.certainty_note: The Second Battle of Tarain is attested in Islamic sources, but the exact date is debated. Most sources place it in 1192, but some argue for 1191 or 1193. The battle's significance is confirmed, but specific details vary between sources.
cross_references: 1206-001 (Muhammad of Ghor's death; establishment of Delhi Sultanate)

events.title: Minamoto Yoritomo establishes Kamakura shogunate; military government in Japan
events.description: Minamoto Yoritomo (1147–1199) established the Kamakura shogunate in 1185 (not 1192, but the shogunate was consolidating its authority in 1192). The shogunate represented a fundamental shift in Japanese governance, from aristocratic (Heian) rule to military (Kamakura) rule. Yoritomo had defeated the Taira clan in 1185 and was now consolidating his military and administrative authority over Japan. In 1192, Yoritomo was strengthening the administrative apparatus of the shogunate and securing his position as the dominant military power in Japan. His system of military governance (bakufu) would outlast him and define Japanese politics for centuries. The establishment of the shogunate is attested in later Japanese chronicles.
key_figures: Minamoto Yoritomo (Shogun, founder of Kamakura shogunate)

sources.name: Azuma Kagami (Mirror of the East), compiled 13th century
Heike Monogatari (Tale of the Heike), oral tradition compiled 13th century

events.certainty_note: Yoritomo's establishment of the shogunate is well-attested in later Japanese chronicles. The exact date of 1185 is given in most sources, but the consolidation of authority in 1192 is less precisely dated.
cross_references: 1185-001 (Minamoto victory at Dan-no-ura; establishment of shogunate)

events.title: Jayavarman VII consolidates Khmer power; military campaigns and temple construction
events.description: Jayavarman VII (r. 1181–1219) was consolidating his power and expanding Khmer territory in 1192. He had recently completed military campaigns to expand Khmer control over neighbouring territories and was beginning major temple construction projects. Jayavarman VII was transforming the Khmer Empire into a Buddhist-dominated state, replacing the earlier Hindu orientation. His reign marked the apogee of Khmer power and cultural achievement. The temple construction projects, including Bayon and Ta Prohm, would become some of the most impressive examples of Khmer architecture. Jayavarman VII's consolidation of power is attested in epigraphic evidence and later historical sources.
key_figures: Jayavarman VII (Khmer king, military conqueror and patron of arts)

sources.name: Epigraphic evidence from Khmer inscriptions at Angkor
Archaeological evidence from Khmer temple complexes
Zhou Daguan, Record of Cambodia (Zhenla Fengtu Ji), written 1297

events.certainty_note: Jayavarman VII's reign and consolidation of power are well-attested. The exact dating of specific military campaigns and temple construction phases in 1192 is less certain, relying on epigraphic evidence and later sources.

events.title: Saladin in declining health; Ayyubid succession preparations
events.description: Saladin (Ṣalāḥ ad-Dīn Yūsuf ibn Ayyūb, 1137–1193) was in declining health in 1192, the year before his death in March 1193. He was preparing for his succession and making arrangements for his sons and emirs to inherit different parts of his empire. Saladin's health decline was noted in contemporary sources, and by 1192 it was clear that his death was imminent. The succession preparations would lead to the fragmentation of the Ayyubid Empire after his death. Saladin's declining health and succession preparations are attested in Islamic chronicles.
key_figures: Saladin (Ṣalāḥ ad-Dīn Yūsuf ibn Ayyūb, Ayyubid sultan)
Al-Afdal Nur ad-Din Ali (Saladin's eldest son, designated successor)

sources.name: Ibn al-Athir, Complete History (al-Kamil fi al-Ta'rikh)
Baha ad-Din ibn Shaddad, Life of Saladin (al-Nawadir al-Sultaniyya)

events.certainty_note: Saladin's declining health in 1192 is attested in Islamic chronicles. The exact nature and severity of his illness are not always clear from the sources, but his death in March 1193 is well-documented.

disconfirming_evidence: Richard's capture (1192-001) is sometimes portrayed as a catastrophic blow to the Angevin position; however, modern scholarship emphasises that while it did weaken Richard's position, the ransom was successfully raised and Richard returned to active campaigning. The Second Battle of Tarain (1192-002) is sometimes portrayed as an immediate and complete victory for Muhammad of Ghor; however, modern scholarship emphasises that the conquest of northern India was a gradual process that took decades. Jayavarman VII's consolidation (1192-004) is sometimes portrayed as immediately triumphant, but modern scholarship emphasises that his consolidation of power took several years and faced significant opposition.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1192 is moderate to good for Europe, the Islamic world, and East Asia, but relies heavily on later compilations and chronicles rather than contemporary records. European sources are predominantly Latin and Norman; Islamic sources are primarily Arabic chronicles written decades or centuries later. Japanese sources are almost entirely from 13th-century compilations (Azuma Kagami, Heike Monogatari), introducing potential anachronism and narrative bias. Indian sources are almost entirely from later compilations (13th century or later), making precise dating difficult. Khmer sources are primarily epigraphic and archaeological, with limited written historical narrative. Sub-Saharan Africa, Northern Europe, and Central Asia are underrepresented in surviving documentation. The record is shaped by the interests of literate elites (courts, monasteries, temples) and may omit significant events in non-literate or non-elite communities.

graph_edges.note: Richard's capture in December 1192 was a consequence of his return from the Third Crusade in 1191.
Richard's capture in 1192 led to his imprisonment and ransom, culminating in his release in February 1194.
The Second Battle of Tarain in 1192 was part of Muhammad of Ghor's broader conquest of northern India.
The Second Battle of Tarain in 1192 led to the establishment of the Delhi Sultanate after Muhammad of Ghor's death in 1206.
Yoritomo's consolidation of the shogunate in 1192 was a continuation of his establishment of the shogunate in 1185.
Saladin's declining health in 1192 led to his death in March 1193 and the fragmentation of the Ayyubid Empire.
